summary: DeVault argues that Firefox and Mozilla have gone from being the jewel of open source to its greatest embarrassment. He catalogs Mozilla's failures: mass layoffs of engineers while raising executive pay, adding ads, siding with Google on antitrust despite financial dependence, and killing projects like Rust and Servo. He concludes that Mozilla will not save the web, that the web itself is a lost cause due to its ever-expanding scope making new browsers impossible to build, and advocates moving to the Gemini protocol as a simpler alternative.
key_points: Mozilla laid off 25% of its workforce (mostly engineers) while executive pay rose 400% as market share dropped 85%
Firefox is the only thing standing between Google and total web monopoly, yet Mozilla has systematically divested from it
The scope of web browsers has grown so recklessly that building a new one is now literally impossible, eliminating any competitive recourse
Mozilla sided with Google on antitrust without disclosing their financial dependence on Google
DeVault advocates abandoning the web entirely in favor of the Gemini protocol, which is simple enough to implement in a weekend
money_quotes: Firefox is the embarrassment of open source, and it's the only thing standing between Google and an all-encompassing monopoly over the web.
Mozilla has divested from Firefox and started funnelling what money is left out of their engineering payroll and into their executive pockets.
The web is dead, and its fetid corpse persists only as the layer of goop that Google scrapes between its servers and your screen.
Anyone who still believes that Mozilla will save the web is a fool.
The scope of web browsers has been increasing at a reckless pace for years, to the point where it's literally impossible to build a new web browser.
Forget about the web, it's a lost cause. Let's move on.
The fuck-up culture is so ingrained in Mozilla in 2020 that it's hard to see it ever getting better again.
key_insight: Mozilla's institutional decay has made Firefox an embarrassment rather than a champion of the open web, and the web's runaway complexity means no alternative browser can arise, leaving abandonment as the only option.
